figures.caption: Table 1. Zero-shot abnormality detection performance comparison on CT-RATE and Rad-ChestCT datasets. † indicates enhanced versions of CT-CLIP further fine-tuned with supervised learning. ‡ indicates the mean performance and standard deviation across five different prompt templates.

Fig. 1. Comparison of VLP paradigms and their corresponding text similarity distributions. (a) Global VLP aligns global visual and report tokens. (b) Fine-grained VLP performs pairwise anatomical alignment but suffers from severe distributional degeneracy (similarity peak near 1.0). (c) CA-GCL (Ours) aggregates anatomy-specific representations into synthetic global tokens while maintaining local alignment, effectively mitigating representation collapse to ensure a discriminative latent space. (d) Zero-shot performance comparison between Fine-grained VLP and CA-GCL.

Fig. 4. t-SNE visualization of image and text embeddings for fVLM (left) and our method (right). While fVLM exhibits significant text embedding collapse, our method effectively separates anatomical clusters in the embedding space.

Table 2. Ablation study on the CT-RATE dataset. We evaluate the individual and cumulative efficacy of Anatomy-aware Local Contrastive Alignment (LCA), ClinicalAware Text Augmentation (CTA), Report Jointly Parsing (RJP), and Cross-Anatomy Global Contrastive Alignment (GCA). Performance is measured by the mean and standard deviation across five distinct prompt templates to assess zero-shot robustness.

Fig. 2. The pipeline of our proposed CA-GCL framework. The framework extracts anatomy-level tokens from CT images and radiology reports (Top). It integrates Anatomy-aware Local Contrastive Alignment for organ-specific matching (BottomLeft) and Cross-anatomy Global Contrastive Alignment (Bottom-Right).
